summary: Gruber dissects Apple's response to the Dutch ACM ruling requiring dating apps to offer alternative payment options. Apple published guidelines charging a 27% commission even when developers use third-party payment processors — only a 3% reduction from the standard 30% — making alternative payment options financially worse for developers. The guidelines appear to deliberately contradict the ACM's requirements by forcing developers to choose one payment method rather than offering both, and include a slanted warning modal that paints non-Apple payments as unsafe. Gruber contrasts Apple's approach with Google's more pragmatic response in South Korea and argues the ACM's focus solely on dating apps is absurd. He concludes that Apple's deliberately unappealing compliance terms seem designed to escalate regulatory tensions rather than resolve them.
key_points: Apple's 27% commission for alternative payment processing leaves developers paying more total fees than the standard 30% through Apple's IAP, since they must also cover third-party payment processing costs on top
Apple's guidelines explicitly contradict the ACM's already-stated requirement that developers must be able to offer both third-party payment and web links simultaneously, not choose one or the other

Google's South Korea approach — requiring third-party payments alongside built-in purchasing with a 4% reduction — is more likely to satisfy regulators than Apple's strategy of making alternatives maximally unappealing
The ACM ruling's exclusive focus on dating apps is logically indefensible, since the arguments about native app necessity apply even more strongly to games, camera apps, and VPNs
Apple has always been clear that its commission is a retailer's cut for the platform, not merely a payment processing fee — many developers and regulators wrongly assumed otherwise
money_quotes: Apple sees the App Store as a store, and the entire iOS third-party ecosystem as an extension of that store, and expects a retailer's cut of sales.
What Apple does say they almost always mean. Just listen to them.
As written, these warnings make Apple look petulant. (Which I suppose is accurate.)
Google's take strikes me as more likely to fly with these regulators.

The whole reason many developers wish they could process their own payments in-app is to pay less in overall fees; with Apple's proposed policy for dating apps in the Netherlands, they'd wind up paying more.
Rather than decrease regulatory tensions, Apple's new guidelines seem to intentionally escalate them.
Paying for stuff in an app, after you've installed it, feels more like something else.
They feel so strongly against Apple's App Store commission that even their thoughts have exclamation marks.
key_insight: Apple's malicious compliance with the Dutch ACM ruling — offering technically alternative payment options that are financially worse for developers — reveals a company more interested in proving a point about its commission model than in resolving regulatory conflict.
